PARAGRAPH 1
To address your request for a robust Python function that filters historical dates by century, we must first establish the precise mathematical definition of a century within the Gregorian calendar system. The standard convention dictates that the first century spans from year 1 to year 100, the second from 101 to 200, and so forth. Consequently, the Nth century encompasses the years from (N minus 1) times 100 plus 1, up to N times 100 inclusive. For instance, the nineteenth century, which you cited as your example, strictly covers the period from 1801 through 1900. This means the year 1800 belongs to the eighteenth century, while 1900 remains in the nineteenth. Many people mistakenly assume that 1900 marks the start of the twentieth century, but mathematically, the twentieth century begins on January 1st, 1901. Your example data confirms this strict interpretation, as the date "1900-01-01" is correctly excluded from the list of nineteenth-century dates in your expected output. With this logic firmly established, we can now construct an efficient algorithm to process your large datasets. The proposed solution will iterate through the provided list of date strings, parse the year component from each entry, and compare it against the calculated start and end years for the target century. To ensure high performance with large lists, we will avoid unnecessary object creation or complex date parsing libraries where simple string slicing suffices. Since the input format is guaranteed to be "YYYY-MM-DD", the year is always the first four characters. We can extract these characters, convert them to an integer, and perform the necessary arithmetic checks. This approach minimizes computational overhead and memory usage, making it ideal for researchers handling massive historical archives. The function will return a new list containing only the matching date strings, preserving their original format. Below is the implementation logic described in detail. We define a function named filter_dates_by_century which accepts the list of dates and the century integer as arguments. Inside the function, we calculate the starting year by taking the century value, subtracting one, multiplying by one hundred, and adding one. The ending year is simply the century multiplied by one hundred. We then initialize an empty list to hold our results. We loop through every date string in the input list.
